title: Psychotria fastigiata
intro: Psychotria fastigiata, also known as Jamaican dogwood, is a perennial plant species native to the Caribbean and Central America, belonging to the Rubiaceae family. It has been traditionally used for its analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and anti-anxiety properties, offering potential relief from various health conditions, including pain management and stress relief. The bioactive constituents of Jamaican dogwood include iridoid glycosides, including ajmalicine and ajugol, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Jamaican dogwood is commonly prepared as a tincture or extract, and its leaves and bark are used in herbal remedies, such as teas and capsules, to harness its health benefits.

It belongs to the genus Psychotria, a diverse group of plants native to tropical and subtropical regions. The species is native to the Americas, specifically in the tropical forests of Central and South America. Within the genus, Psychotria fastigiata is typically classified as a shrub or small tree. uses: Psychotria fastigiata has been traditionally used in folk medicine in various regions, including Southeast Asia, where it is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. In some cultures, the plant is used to treat fever, coughs, and respiratory issues. The leaves and roots of Psychotria fastigiata are often used in teas or infusions to aid in digestion and relieve stomach discomfort. Additionally, the plant is thought to have antibacterial properties, making it useful for wound care and skin infections. More research is needed to fully understand the medicinal properties and uses of Psychotria fastigiata.
